sections.title: Time Words
sections.explanation: Chinese has a clear set of time words organized into logical groups. Days: 昨天 (zuótiān, yesterday), 今天 (jīntiān, today), 明天 (míngtiān, tomorrow). Relative time: 现在 (xiànzài, now), 以前 (yǐqián, before/in the past), 以后 (yǐhòu, after/in the future). Parts of the day: 早上 (zǎoshang, morning), 中午 (zhōngwǔ, noon), 下午 (xiàwǔ, afternoon), 晚上 (wǎnshang, evening/night). Notice how 上 means 'upper/first part' and 下 means 'lower/second part' — morning is the upper half, afternoon is the lower half of the day.
sections.tip: The character 天 (tiān) means 'day' or 'sky'. You can see it in 昨天, 今天, 明天 — just swap the first character to shift between yesterday, today, and tomorrow.

examples.en: It is 8:00 in the morning now.

exercises.explanation: 明天 (míngtiān) means 'tomorrow'. 昨天 = yesterday, 今天 = today.

exercises.prompt: Match each time word to its meaning:
exercises.explanation: 早上 = morning, 中午 = noon, 下午 = afternoon, 晚上 = evening/night.

exercises.hint: The word for 'yesterday' starts with 昨

sections.title: Time-First Word Order
sections.explanation: In Chinese, time expressions come BEFORE the verb, typically at the beginning of the sentence or right after the subject. The standard order is: Subject + Time + Verb (+ Object). For example, '我明天去' (I tomorrow go = I will go tomorrow). This is the opposite of English, where time usually goes at the end. Time can also go before the subject for emphasis: '明天我去' (Tomorrow I go). Both positions are correct, but time NEVER goes after the verb the way it does in English.
sections.tip: Think of Chinese word order as: WHO + WHEN + does WHAT. Time sets the scene before the action happens — the listener knows the time frame before hearing the verb.

examples.en: I will go to Beijing tomorrow.
We will have a meeting at 3 PM.
He is not at home now.

exercises.prompt: Where does the time word go in a Chinese sentence?
options: Before the verb (after the subject or at the start)
After the verb, like in English
At the very end of the sentence
exercises.explanation: In Chinese, time expressions come before the verb. The pattern is Subject + Time + Verb (+ Object).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'I will go tomorrow'?
exercises.explanation: 我明天去。— Subject (我) + Time (明天) + Verb (去). Time must come before the verb.

exercises.prompt: 她___看电影。(She will watch a movie tonight.) — use 晚上
exercises.hint: Place the time word before the verb 看
exercises.explanation: 她晚上看电影。Time (晚上) goes between the subject (她) and the verb (看).

sections.title: Duration
sections.explanation: To express how long an action has lasted, Chinese uses 了 (le) after the verb, followed by the duration. The pattern is: Verb + 了 + Duration (+ Object). For example, '学了两年' (studied for two years). If there is an object, the duration goes between 了 and the object, or you can repeat the verb: '我学了两年中文' or '我学中文学了两年'. The pattern 从...到... (cóng...dào...) means 'from...to...' and is used for time ranges: '从九点到五点' (from 9 o'clock to 5 o'clock).
sections.tip: 了 after the verb signals that the action has a measured result — here, a duration. Think of it as 'verb + marker + how long'.

examples.en: I have studied Chinese for two years.
I work from 9 o'clock to 5 o'clock.

examples.zh: 她从星期一到星期五上班。
examples.en: She works from Monday to Friday.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I studied for three hours'?
exercises.explanation: Verb (学) + 了 + Duration (三个小时). The duration follows 了 after the verb.

exercises.prompt: 我从八点___六点上课。(I have class from 8 to 6.) — fill in the linking word
exercises.explanation: 从...到... is the 'from...to...' pattern. 从八点到六点 = from 8 to 6.

exercises.prompt: What does 了 signal in '他住了五年'?
options: A future plan to live somewhere
A question about how long he lived
The duration of the action (he lived for five years)
exercises.explanation: Verb + 了 + duration indicates how long an action lasted. 他住了五年 = He lived (there) for five years.

sections.title: Frequency
sections.explanation: Frequency words express how often something happens. Common ones include: 每天 (měitiān, every day), 经常 (jīngcháng, often), 有时候 (yǒu shíhou, sometimes), 很少 (hěn shǎo, rarely), 从来不 (cónglái bù, never). Like other time expressions, frequency words go BEFORE the verb: '我每天跑步' (I run every day). 从来不 is a strong negation meaning 'never in one's life': '他从来不喝酒' (He never drinks alcohol). 每 (měi) means 'every' and combines with time nouns: 每天 (every day), 每年 (every year), 每个星期 (every week).
sections.tip: Frequency words follow the same rule as all time expressions — they go before the verb. Never put them at the end of the sentence.

exercises.prompt: Match each frequency word to its meaning:
exercises.explanation: 每天 = every day, 经常 = often, 有时候 = sometimes, 从来不 = never.

exercises.prompt: Where does 经常 go in 'She often reads books' (她/经常/看书)?
options: 经常她看书。(before the subject only)
exercises.explanation: Frequency adverbs go after the subject and before the verb: 她经常看书。

exercises.prompt: 他___喝咖啡。(He never drinks coffee.) — use the phrase meaning 'never'
exercises.explanation: 从来不 means 'never'. 他从来不喝咖啡。The phrase goes directly before the verb.
